title: Learning Agents
concept: Agents that modify their behavior based on success or failure critiques from previous runs.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: a utility-based agent picks its next move by...

options.text: Scoring several possible paths and choosing the one with the best cost/benefit
Always taking the very first option it sees

exercises.explain: A utility-based agent compares options using a 'utility' score (like cost, speed, or quality) and picks the best one. It weighs choices instead of guessing.

cards.title: Getting Better Each Try
cards.body: Ever failed a game level, then beat it next time because you learned the trap? Some AI agents work the same way. They keep notes on what went wrong and improve on the next run.

cards.title: What Is a Learning Agent?
cards.body: A learning agent changes its own behavior based on feedback from past runs. It looks at what succeeded or failed, then adjusts its choices to do better next time.

cards.title: Like Practicing Free Throws
cards.body: You shoot, miss to the left, and aim a bit right next time. A learning agent does this too: it uses each 'miss' as a critique and nudges its next attempt closer to success.

cards.title: In Action
cards.body: An email-writing agent sends a draft, gets a 'too long, be shorter' critique, and remembers that. Next time it writes tighter emails without being told again.

cards.title: Myth: It Learns Instantly and Perfectly
cards.body: Not true. A learning agent improves gradually from feedback, and it can still repeat mistakes. Learning takes many tries, just like for people.

cards.title: Fixed vs. Learning
cards.body: A basic agent repeats the same mistake forever. A learning agent uses a critique to change course next run.

left.text: Fails, then fails the exact same way again

right.text: Fails, reads the critique, then adjusts and improves

exercises.prompt: What makes an agent a 'learning agent'?

options.text: It modifies its behavior based on success/failure feedback from past runs
It never changes what it does
It only works if a human retypes the whole task
It picks answers completely at random

exercises.explain: The defining trait is using critiques from previous runs to adjust future behavior, so it gets better over time instead of repeating mistakes.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A learning agent usually improves gradually over many tries, not perfectly on the first try.
exercises.explain: Learning agents improve step by step from feedback. They can still make mistakes, and getting better takes repeated attempts.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about how learning agents improve.
exercises.sentence: A learning agent uses a critique from a past run to adjust its behavior next time.
exercises.explain: The critique is the feedback about what went right or wrong. The agent uses it to change its future choices.

exercises.prompt: Which of these was written by an AI learning agent describing how it improved?

options.text: Run 1 was flagged as too wordy; I stored that note and produced a shorter, clearer draft on run 2.
i dont know just do it however you want lol
Meeting moved to 3pm, see you there!

exercises.explain: The AI learning agent references a prior critique ('too wordy') and explains how it adjusted its next run. That self-correcting loop is the giveaway.

exercises.prompt: A coding agent writes a program, the tests fail, and it reads the error, fixes the bug, and passes on the next run. Which agent archetype is this, and why?

options.text: A learning agent, because it used the failure critique from the last run to change its behavior and improve
A utility-based agent, because it scored many paths before acting
A simple reflex agent, because it just reacts to the current input with a fixed rule
A random agent, because it guessed the fix

exercises.explain: The key clue is that it took feedback (the failed test) from a previous run and used it to adjust and improve the next attempt. That self-improvement loop is exactly what defines a learning agent, setting it apart from utility-based scoring or fixed reflex rules.
